script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and this is The Portfolio Podcast. Friday's market close — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio ended the session at ninety-eight thousand four hundred forty-four dollars and thirty-one cents, down one thousand five hundred fifty-five dollars and sixty-nine cents on the day. That is a loss of one point five six percent. A rough Friday for tech-heavy exposure.

Broadcom, A-V-G-O, dropped four point four six percent at Friday's close. That is the single worst performer in this portfolio on the session, and it was not close. Seventeen dollars and five cents erased in one day, closing at three hundred sixty-five dollars and two cents. When a name carrying nearly five percent of portfolio weight moves like that, it pulls the whole allocation lower.

Futures pointed higher heading into Friday's open, lifted in part by reports of a halt to United States and Iranian military exchanges and early signals of peace talks. That geopolitical relief bid gave the broader market an initial tailwind. The Nasdaq Composite gave back those gains through the afternoon session, finishing the day in the red as selling pressure in large-cap technology overwhelmed any macro optimism. No major Federal Reserve commentary or economic data releases drove the tape on Friday. The session's story was concentrated in individual names, not the macro backdrop.

The top story Friday belongs to Apple, A-A-P-L. Bank of America Securities released a note following Apple's twenty twenty-six Worldwide Developers Conference, maintaining its Buy rating with a price objective of three hundred eighty dollars. The analysts argued that investors are materially underestimating the significance of Apple's revamped Siri artificial intelligence architecture. According to the note, the new AI strategy represents a positive reset for a company that has faced consistent criticism for falling behind in the AI race. The brokerage's view is that the updated platform could strengthen Apple's ecosystem and support a meaningful hardware upgrade cycle heading into the next iPhone generation. That is a bullish fundamental case. The market did not agree on Friday. Apple, A-A-P-L, closed at two hundred eighty-three dollars and seventy-eight cents, down nine dollars and thirty cents, a decline of three point one seven percent on the session. The stock remains well below Bank of America's three hundred eighty dollar target, which means there is a substantial gap between where the shares trade and where the analyst community's most vocal bull sees fair value. The WWDC narrative and the AI reset thesis will be the dominant framework for Apple through the summer. Whether the market begins to price in that upgrade cycle is the key question heading into the back half of twenty twenty-six.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, moving from largest to smallest portfolio weight.

Microsoft, M-S-F-T, was the one bright spot in large-cap technology at Friday's close. The stock gained seven dollars and fifty-one cents, closing at three hundred seventy-two dollars and ninety-seven cents, up two point zero five percent on the session. That is a meaningful divergence from the rest of the tech complex. Context matters here, though. A separate headline circulating Friday noted that Microsoft, M-S-F-T, is on pace for its worst monthly performance since December two thousand, with shares down over twenty percent in June alone. The market cap has fallen from roughly four trillion dollars twelve months ago to approximately two point six five trillion dollars as of Friday's close. One day of gains does not reverse that trend. On the positive side, CoreWeave disclosed a ninety-nine billion dollar backlog tied to major artificial intelligence infrastructure contracts, with Microsoft, M-S-F-T, listed as a key partner alongside Nvidia, Meta, and OpenAI. That long-term AI infrastructure commitment is a data point bulls will point to as evidence that enterprise AI spending remains intact. The June drawdown and the AI backlog are in direct tension. Watch for any forward guidance commentary or management signals in the weeks ahead.

Nvidia, N-V-D-A, closed at one hundred ninety-two dollars and fifty-three cents, down six dollars and forty-seven cents, a decline of three point two five percent on Friday. That is the second-largest percentage loss in the portfolio on the session. OpenAI is reportedly considering delaying its initial public offering, according to a report published Friday evening. Nvidia, N-V-D-A, as a core supplier of GPU infrastructure to OpenAI and other AI labs, carries indirect exposure to sentiment around AI commercialization timelines. Any slowdown in that narrative creates headline risk. The CoreWeave backlog news, which includes Nvidia as a named partner, provides some counterbalance. The stock remains a central position in Q-Q-Q-M at eight point one five percent of the portfolio. The AI buildout thesis is intact structurally, but Friday's price action reflects near-term uncertainty.

Alphabet, G-O-O-G-L, closed at three hundred thirty-seven dollars and thirty-nine cents, down seven dollars and ninety cents, off two point two nine percent on the session. Analysts at one outlet made the case Friday that Alphabet's recent pullback puts the name in bargain territory relative to megacap peers. The geopolitical backdrop — specifically the reported halt to United States-Iranian hostilities — was cited as a broad catalyst that lifted futures but did not fully translate into Friday's close for the name. G-O-O-G-L carries eight point zero one percent of this portfolio. The dip thesis is out there. Price action will need to confirm it.

Amazon, A-M-Z-N, closed at two hundred thirty-two dollars and sixty-nine cents, down one dollar and fifty-eight cents, a decline of zero point six seven percent on Friday. The most modest loss among the decliners in this portfolio. No stock-specific news drove the move. Amazon, A-M-Z-N, represents five point five two percent of this portfolio. Its relative outperformance on a down day for tech is worth noting. Cloud and consumer discretionary pressures remain the framework to watch.

Over at Broadcom, A-V-G-O — as noted at the top — closed at three hundred sixty-five dollars and two cents, down seventeen dollars and five cents, a loss of four point four six percent. That is the worst single-session performance in the portfolio on Friday. No major company-specific catalyst was identifiable for the move. The selloff appears tied to broad semiconductor pressure. At four point six one percent of portfolio weight, this hit landed hard.

Meta Platforms, M-E-T-A, closed at five hundred fifty dollars and twenty-five cents, down seven dollars and forty-two cents, off one point three three percent on the session. Meta, M-E-T-A, is named as a partner in CoreWeave's ninety-nine billion dollar AI infrastructure backlog. That is a long-term positive for capital deployment signaling. Friday's price action, however, reflected the broader tech selloff. Meta carries four point four eight percent of this portfolio.

Tesla, T-S-L-A, was one of two names to finish Friday in positive territory. The stock closed at three hundred seventy-nine dollars and seventy-one cents, up four dollars and eighteen cents, a gain of one point one one percent. A report circulating Friday noted that Tesla's second-quarter delivery numbers are due in the first days of July, and analysts flagged that the delivery count is the figure that matters most right now — more than any robotaxi update. Tesla, T-S-L-A, represents three point five four percent of this portfolio. That delivery print is the near-term catalyst.

Costco, C-O-S-T, closed at nine hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-four cents, down eight dollars and fifty-five cents, a decline of zero point eight nine percent on the session. No company-specific news on Friday. Costco, C-O-S-T, as a consumer staples name in an otherwise technology-heavy portfolio, held up relatively well against the broader selling pressure. At two point eight one percent portfolio weight, the move was contained. Membership and consumer spending data remain the macro inputs to watch for this name.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Tesla's, T-S-L-A's, second-quarter delivery report, expected in the first days of July — analysts have flagged this as the most important near-term data point for the stock. Second, any further developments on the OpenAI initial public offering timeline — a delay would recalibrate sentiment across AI-exposed names including Nvidia, N-V-D-A, and Microsoft, M-S-F-T. Third, next week's jobs report and consumer confidence data — macro prints that will frame Federal Reserve expectations and risk appetite heading into July for all Q-Q-Q-M holdings.

That is your Friday close recap. The next episode drops at five PM Eastern Time on the next trading day. Watch the Tesla delivery number — it is going to set the tone for consumer discretionary and EV sentiment heading into the holiday week. I am Josh Weinberg. This is The Portfolio Podcast.
